text: In this technical video, we present robotic ventral mesh rectopexy. Robotic surgery for pelvic organ prolapse repair has been increasingly used in recent years. The technical advantages of the robot are three-dimensional magnification, articulating instruments with seven degrees of freedom, excellent ergonomics, motion scaling, tremor-free movements, and enhanced intracorporeal steering capability. In our institution, since two thousand eight, all one hundred robotic mesh rectopexies have been performed. We use a modified dehaut rectopexy and levotoropexy with slight modifications.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the pelvic floor muscles on either side of the upper anal canal, ensuring sutures are not placed directly into the rectal wall.

The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position for the procedure. A 10mm conventional laparoscopic trocar is placed at the umbilicus, along with three 8mm robotic trocars and a 12mm conventional laparoscopic trocar on the patient's left side. The robotic system is docked between the patient's legs, with the surgical assistant standing on the left side.

The surgeon checks the robotic arms to ensure they do not collide, maintaining optimal range of motion. Dissection begins on the right lateral peritoneum at the sacral promontory level, opening the peritoneum along the pelvis. The dissection progresses into the rectovaginal septum, aided by a vaginal retractor for better exposure. Both sharp and blunt dissection techniques are employed. The dissection reaches the distal rectum, exposing the levator ani muscle at the upper anal canal, approximately two to three centimeters above the dentate line.

During the dissection of the rectovaginal septum, traction and countertraction are applied. Fatty tissue is left on the posterior surface of the vagina to provide an extra layer preventing mesh erosion. The lateral stalks of the rectum and the posterior surface of the mesorectum are not dissected, leaving the rectum in the presacral space. A non-resorbable L-shaped Marcelin mesh is cut and applied anteriorly over the rectum, with its long limb lying on the right side. The mesh is interposed between the rectum and vagina, and the lower end is sutured to the levator muscles on either side of the rectum, avoiding direct suturing on the rectum to prevent mesh erosion.

The surgeon dissects an avascular area over the sacral promontory and anterior prevertebral ligament. The mesh is fixed to the sacral promontory using one or two stitches without tension. An additional suture secures the upper part of the vagina to the mesh, obliterating the Douglas pouch. The peritoneum is then closed over the mesh.

At first post-operative day, oral intake is started and bladder catheter is removed and patients are usually discharged post-operative day three. In our approach of rectopexy, important surgical steps have been facilitated by the robotic assistants. First, achieving complete dissection of rectovaginal septum. Second, gentle dissection with respect to lateral stults of the rectum and posterior rectal wall. Third, sturing of mesh to levator muscles on both sides of the anal canal.
